name: Azelaic acid
lead: The molecule that treats acne, rosacea, and pigment from a single jar — without bleaching towels, drying skin, or being banned in pregnancy. The fact that you have not heard of it the way you have heard of retinol is a marketing failure, not a clinical one.

whatItIs.body: zelaic acid is a saturated, nine-carbon dicarboxylic acid produced by the yeast Malassezia furfur, the same skin-resident organism implicated in pityriasis and fungal acne. The body has been making and tolerating azelaic acid on the skin's surface for millennia; topical formulations replicate that exposure at therapeutic doses. The result is a molecule with antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, comedolytic, and tyrosinase-inhibiting effects all from a single application — a pharmacological elegance no other OTC active matches.
whatItIs.body2: The reason it is under-prescribed in India is partly cultural — patients want "lightening creams" not anti-inflammatories — and partly pharmacy economics: at ₹400 a tube, the marketing budget is small.

mechanism.b: Selectively suppresses C. acnes proliferation in the follicle. Reaches BPO-comparable endpoints in some RCTs.
Reduces ROS in keratinocytes; calms papular rosacea. The endpoint dermatologists actually prescribe it for.
Selectively targets hyperactive melanocytes (i.e., PIH, melasma) without affecting baseline pigment.

evidence.note: 20% azelaic reaches BPO efficacy without the bleach-and-burn profile.
Slower than adapalene; useful in adapalene-intolerant patients.
First-line in many derm guidelines. The molecule rosacea was waiting for.
Comparable endpoints to hydroquinone in some 24-week trials.

concentration.b: Adequate for mild–moderate acne and most PIH. Twice-daily, 12 weeks.
Reference for papulopustular rosacea. Foam or gel vehicle.
Strongest documented efficacy for melasma. Slower vehicle, more pilling.
Not necessary in most cases. The OTC and Rx ladder covers the use cases.

products.name: Azelaic Acid Suspension 10%
products.note: Cheapest serious azelaic in India. Twice-daily for 12 weeks.

products.name: 10% Azelaic Acid Booster
products.note: Better vehicle, less pilling. Worth the premium for sensitive skin.

products.name: Sébium AKN
products.note: Azelaic + niacinamide combo. Convenient single-step option.

products.name: Finacea Foam 15% (Rx)
products.note: Rx-strength reference for rosacea. Foam vehicle is genuinely elegant.

faq.q: Why does it tingle for the first week?
faq.a: Azelaic at clinical doses produces transient burning or tingling for 5–10 minutes after application — it's a pharmacological signature, not damage. Almost universal in week one, fades by week three. If it persists past four weeks, switch vehicle (foam over gel) or move to alternate-day.
For acne and PIH, yes — particularly twice-daily for the full 12 weeks. For severe rosacea or melasma, the 15–20% prescription forms are meaningfully stronger and worth the consultation.

faq.q: Can I use it with retinoids?
faq.a: Yes — among the most reliable pairings we recommend. Azelaic morning, retinoid evening. Or alternate nights if your skin tolerates both poorly. The mechanisms complement; the irritation does not stack much.
Yes — one of very few actives with clear pregnancy-safe data. Often the only effective choice when retinoids and tranexamic acid are off the table.
